title: The Strategic-Management Process; Vision & Mission
simple_html:
Strategic management is how an organization decides where it wants to go and how it will get there better than its rivals. It is not a one-time plan but an ongoing cycle of thinking, choosing, doing, and checking.

The big idea

Every firm has limited money, people, and time. Strategy is the set of choices about where to compete and how to win, so those limited resources create the most value. Without strategy, a company drifts and reacts; with it, the company acts on purpose.

The basic cycle

Most textbooks describe three stages: formulation (analyze the situation and pick a direction), implementation (turn the plan into action, budgets, and structure), and evaluation (measure results and correct course). It is continuous because the world keeps changing.

Vision vs. mission

A vision statement answers "What do we want to become?" — it is future-focused and inspiring. A mission statement answers "What is our business, and whom do we serve?" — it describes the present purpose, customers, products, and values. Vision points to the destination; mission describes the daily journey.

Key takeaways

  • Strategy = deliberate choices about where to play and how to win.
  • The process is a continuous loop: formulate, implement, evaluate.
  • Vision is the long-term aspiration; mission is the present purpose.
  • Good statements guide real decisions, not just decorate a wall.
  • Strategy aligns everyone so scarce resources are not wasted.
intermediate_html:
Strategic management is the art and science of formulating, implementing, and evaluating cross-functional decisions that enable an organization to achieve its objectives. It integrates management, marketing, finance, operations, R&D, and information systems to gain and sustain competitive advantage.

The three-stage model

The widely taught model (associated with Fred David) has three stages:

  1. Strategy formulation — develop the vision and mission, conduct external analysis (opportunities and threats) and internal analysis (strengths and weaknesses), set long-term objectives, generate alternatives, and choose strategies to pursue.
  2. Strategy implementation — set annual objectives, craft policies, motivate employees, and allocate resources so strategies can be executed. Often called the "action stage," it requires discipline, structure, and culture alignment.
  3. Strategy evaluation — review internal and external factors, measure performance, and take corrective action. Because conditions change, today's success is no guarantee of tomorrow's.

Vision and mission as the foundation

A vision statement is short and answers "What do we want to become?" A mission statement is broader and answers "What is our business?" Effective mission statements typically address up to nine components: customers, products/services, markets, technology, concern for survival/growth/profitability, philosophy, self-concept, concern for public image, and concern for employees.

Worked mini-case

Imagine a regional coffee chain. Vision: "To be the most loved neighborhood coffee experience in the country." Mission: "We serve ethically sourced coffee and fresh food to busy professionals in urban centers (customers, products, markets), using locally roasted beans (technology/self-concept), while treating our baristas as partners (employees) and reducing waste (public image)." Notice the mission is concrete enough to guide decisions — it tells managers to invest in sourcing, training, and urban locations, and to reject, say, a rural drive-through expansion.

How to apply it

  • Start with mission and vision before analysis — they set the boundaries of "acceptable" strategies.
  • Make objectives long-term, measurable, and consistent with the mission (e.g., 15% revenue growth over three years).
  • Treat the process as iterative: evaluation feeds back into the next round of formulation.
  • Involve managers across functions — strategy is cross-functional, not just a CEO exercise.

Why it matters for the exam

Examiners love the formulate-implement-evaluate sequence and the distinction between vision (become) and mission (business now). Be ready to critique a weak mission statement against the nine components, and to explain why implementation is the hardest stage — most strategies fail not in design but in execution.
full_html:
Strategic management is the integrative discipline concerned with how organizations create and sustain superior performance over time. Formally, it is the formulation, implementation, and evaluation of cross-functional decisions that enable an organization to achieve its objectives (David & David, 2017). It sits at the apex of management because it synthesizes the functional areas — marketing, finance, operations, R&D, and information systems — into a coherent direction. This opening lesson establishes the process model and the foundational artifacts of vision and mission that frame every subsequent stage in the syllabus.

Defining strategy: deliberate and emergent

Classic definitions emphasize deliberate choice: strategy as the determination of long-run goals and the allocation of resources to achieve them (Chandler, 1962), or as the creation of a unique and valuable position involving a different set of activities (Porter, 1996). Yet strategy is not purely top-down. Mintzberg (1978) distinguished intended strategy (planned) from realized strategy (what actually happens), introducing the notion of emergent strategy — patterns that form without prior intention as managers respond to events. Realized strategy is thus a blend of deliberate plans and emergent learning. This nuance matters: a rigid formal plan that ignores emergent signals can be as dangerous as having no plan at all.

The strategic-management process model

The dominant pedagogical model (David & David, 2017) frames the process as three continuous, interdependent stages:

  1. Formulation — developing vision and mission; performing external audit (opportunities and threats via PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces, the EFE and CPM matrices — see Lesson 2) and internal audit (strengths and weaknesses via RBV/VRIO, the value chain, and the IFE matrix — see Lesson 3); establishing long-term objectives; generating, evaluating, and selecting strategies (using the matching and decision tools of Lesson 6).
  2. Implementation — setting annual objectives, devising policies, allocating resources, aligning structure and culture, and motivating people (Lesson 7). This is the "action stage" and is consistently the hardest; even brilliant strategies fail when execution falters.
  3. Evaluation — reviewing the bases of strategy, measuring performance against expectations, and taking corrective action (Lesson 8), increasingly via the Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton, 1992).

The feedback arrows are essential: evaluation re-informs formulation, making the model a learning loop rather than a linear pipeline. An alternative lens, the design/positioning/learning schools catalogued by Mintzberg, Ahlstrand, and Lampel (1998) in Strategy Safari, reminds us that this rational-analytic model is only one of several legitimate ways to conceive of strategy formation.

Vision and mission

The vision statement answers "What do we want to become?" It is aspirational, succinct, and time-horizoned. The mission statement answers "What is our business?" — a question Drucker (1973) argued is the single most important and most neglected in management, because the answer determines what the organization will and will not do. David & David (2017) propose that strong mission statements address up to nine components: customers; products or services; markets; technology; concern for survival, growth, and profitability; philosophy; self-concept (distinctive competence); concern for public image; and concern for employees. Collins and Porras (1996) complement this with the idea of a core ideology (core values plus core purpose) paired with an envisioned future built around a "Big Hairy Audacious Goal."

Worked example

Consider a hospital network. A weak mission — "We provide quality healthcare" — fails almost every component. A strong version: "We deliver accessible, evidence-based care to families across the southwest region (customers, markets), spanning emergency, surgical, and preventive services (products), using integrated electronic health records and tele-medicine (technology), while remaining financially sustainable (profitability), guided by compassion and equity (philosophy), recognized as the region's most trusted provider (public image, self-concept), and powered by valued clinical teams (employees)." This version actively rules out, say, a luxury cosmetic-surgery line that conflicts with the equity philosophy — illustrating that a mission's real test is the decisions it constrains.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Confusing vision and mission — vision = future aspiration; mission = present scope and purpose.
  • Treating statements as decoration — if no decision is ever rejected because of the mission, it is not doing strategic work.
  • Equating strategy with a document — strategy is a pattern of action (Mintzberg, 1978), not a binder.
  • Over-relying on deliberate planning — ignoring emergent learning in turbulent environments invites failure.
  • Skipping evaluation — "set and forget" defeats the continuous nature of the process.

Debates and nuance

A long-standing debate contrasts the planning/design school (strategy as a controlled, conscious process) with the learning school (strategy as an emergent, adaptive process). Ansoff (1965) championed formal planning; Mintzberg (1994), in The Rise and Fall of Strategic Planning, critiqued its "fallacies of predetermination, detachment, and formalization." The pragmatic synthesis: use formal analysis to discipline thinking, but stay alert to emergent signals and revise. This tension recurs throughout the course — most directly in the contrast between rigorous matrix tools (Lesson 6) and the cultural, behavioral realities of implementation (Lesson 7).

Cross-links

This lesson is the spine of the syllabus. Formulation feeds directly into External Analysis (Lesson 2) and Internal Analysis (Lesson 3); the choices made flow into Business-Level (Lesson 4) and Corporate (Lesson 5) strategy; selection is operationalized in Strategy-Formulation Matrices (Lesson 6); and the loop closes through Implementation (Lesson 7) and Evaluation and Governance (Lesson 8).
